<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: project-theseus-as-report-first-implementation-reference
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/project-theseus-as-report-first-implementation-reference.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/project-theseus-as-report-first-implementation-reference.qmd@9ea087fd0
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Project Theseus as Report-First Implementation Reference

An architecture becomes more serious when it has to explain itself through
records.

That is the role Project Theseus plays in this book. Theseus is not used here
as proof that the ASI Stack already works. It is used as an implementation
reference: a nearby system that makes the book ask what a governed,
self-improving AI project would have to leave behind if it wanted to be
inspectable after the fact.

A dashboard can make a project feel alive. It can show green checks, queued
work, current pressure, and apparent progress. But a dashboard is not the
evidence object. The evidence object is the report bundle underneath it: the
contract, route, work item, gate, residual, checksum, replay note, missing
artifact, and non-claim that survive when the screen is closed.

Project Theseus matters because it pressures the ASI Stack toward that kind of
record. It gives the book a concrete way to think about plans, gates, ledgers,
work boards, checkpoints, and self-evolution controls without letting private
or source-reported implementation status become public empirical proof.

The whole reference can be reduced to one discipline: if a claim depends on a
run, the report has to carry it.

## Problem

The ASI Stack cannot remain only a set of conceptual layers.

Intent, governance, planning, memory, routing, execution, evidence, benchmarks,
and self-improvement all sound plausible when they are drawn separately. The
harder question is what happens when a system actually tries to operate them
together. Which goal was admitted? Which plan was compiled? Which route was
chosen? Which work item changed? Which gate passed or failed? Which residual
remained? Which artifact can a reviewer inspect later?

Project Theseus gives the book an implementation reference for those
questions. Its source notes describe report-first machinery: pressure loops,
plan compiler contracts, architecture gates, residual escrow, operator work
boards, checkpoints, and self-evolution controls.

The boundary is as important as the reference. The public book cannot claim
current Theseus benchmark performance, training readiness, dashboard state,
self-evolution safety, model quality, or deployment readiness unless the
relevant reports, commands, environments, and publication permissions are
present here. They are not.

That makes Theseus more useful, not less. The point is not to say, "a prototype
exists somewhere, therefore the architecture is proven." The point is to ask
what a prototype would have to report before anyone should trust it.

## Why Dashboards Do Not Count

A prototype roadmap is not enough. Neither is a screenshot, status page, or
confident summary.

Self-improving systems are especially vulnerable to narrative overclaim. The
tempting story is always that the loop improved itself. The governed story has
to be more boring and more exact:

- what pressure entered the system;
- what authority allowed the attempt;
- what artifact changed;
- what gate accepted, narrowed, or blocked the change;
- what residual survived;
- what future reviewer can inspect.

Dashboards can help operators see those facts, but dashboards should not be
where the facts live. The report bundle is the authority surface. It should
outlive the run, name its own limits, and make missing artifacts visible.

This is where Theseus connects to adjacent reporting disciplines. Model cards,
dataset datasheets, AI service FactSheets, and machine-learning
reproducibility checklists all push toward structured disclosure, provenance,
intended-use boundaries, limitation reporting, and artifact review. Theseus
report packets would need those ideas plus additional fields for gates,
residuals, work-board entries, replay state, publication boundaries, and
non-claims. The comparison is orienting only; this repository has not produced
or reproduced those external artifacts for Theseus.

## Core Claim

Project Theseus should be mined as a report-first implementation reference for
RMI, showing how pressure, residuals, gates, ledgers, operator work boards,
checkpoints, and self-evolution governance can instantiate the ASI Stack
without promoting local results as general capability claims.

In the live book, that claim remains a design rationale at `argument` support.

The source notes are strong enough to guide design discussion. They are not
enough to promote the Project Theseus core claim. The separate Project Theseus
imports add two static, digest-verified report fixtures and one local support
replay probe over the ASI-side validators; they do not give this repository a
clean live Theseus replay, reproduced benchmark run, current dashboard
verification, private workload publication, or model-quality result.

The distinction is the main contribution here. Theseus can teach the book what
evidence should look like while the book refuses to claim that the evidence
already proves more than it does.

## The Report Loop

The implementation pattern is a loop:

1. pressure enters as a goal, benchmark, operator task, or unresolved residual;
2. a plan compiler turns that pressure into a contract and semantic work
   structure;
3. routing selects bounded machinery to attempt the work;
4. execution emits artifacts, reports, logs, and work-board updates;
5. gates decide what can be accepted, narrowed, blocked, quarantined, or sent
   back for review;
6. residuals become the next pressure instead of disappearing.

In Theseus terms, the useful artifact is not the fact that the loop exists. The
useful artifact is the record showing what happened at each step.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  Pressure["Goal, benchmark, task, or residual"] --> Contract["Plan contract and semantic IR"]
  Contract --> Route["Bounded route and tool choice"]
  Route --> Work["Work item, arm, tool, or Hive task"]
  Work --> Bundle["Report bundle"]
  Bundle --> Gate["Architecture, resource, safety, or promotion gate"]
  Gate --> Decision["Accept, narrow, block, quarantine, or review"]
  Decision --> Residual["Residual and next pressure"]
  Residual --> Pressure

  Bundle --> Boundary["Public claim boundary"]
  Boundary --> Missing["Missing artifact row"]
  Boundary --> NonClaim["Non-claims"]
  Boundary -. "prevents" .-> Overclaim["Dashboard or local status becoming public proof"]
```

The loop is governed only if the blocked path is as visible as the successful
path. A missing command, dirty checkout, absent checksum, private payload, or
unreplayed report should not be smoothed over. It should become a row that
prevents stronger claims.

## What The Static Imports Mean

The ASI Stack repository currently contains two public-safe Project Theseus
report import lanes, one support replay probe, and one report-bundle audit.

The first import is a sanitized architecture-gate summary. It records a
checkpointed 14 of 14 gate summary with zero external inference calls, verifies
the pinned source-artifact digest, and rejects invalid mutations for digest
mismatch, private-payload copying, and support-promotion overclaim.

That is useful. It proves that this repository can hold a public-safe Theseus
report contract, verify a digest boundary, and reject a few obvious forms of
evidence laundering.

The second import is a sanitized generation-mode summary. It records 18 modes,
13 comparisons, zero hard gaps, zero promotable comparisons, and useful
solution per second at `0.0`. That result is not embarrassing; it is the point.
The gate can observe accepted-span speed lift and still refuse promotion when
verified task success remains zero.

The support replay probe then reruns the two ASI-side validators, records
command-output digests, checks ten tracked artifacts, and preserves support-
state effect `none`. It does not create a support-state transition.

The report-bundle audit checks the bookkeeping shape that a stronger Theseus
packet would need. It validates one public-safe repository fixture with seven
expected-invalid controls, two replay-ready rows, one blocked replay row, eight
stack-layer crosswalk rows, five gate mappings, six visible artifact gaps, and
six self-evolution intervention-ladder levels. It is a useful audit because it
keeps the missing pieces visible: the clean live replay, public task bundle,
current work-board state, benchmark environment notes, publication permissions,
and external review are still absent.

The whole surface is narrow. It does not rerun Project Theseus. It does not
prove that the current checkout is clean. It does not reproduce a benchmark. It
does not inspect a live dashboard. It does not authorize training. It does not
prove deployed Theseus runtime behavior, generation speed,
useful-solution-per-second improvement, model quality, routing quality,
benchmark quality, safety, alignment, transfer, or ASI. It does not move the
Project Theseus core claim above `argument`.

The right lesson is procedural: the report lane is promising because it can say
no. It can reject private payload leakage, digest drift, and support-state
overclaim before those errors become prose.

## Interfaces

The Theseus reference connects to the ASI Stack through reportable interfaces:

- plan compiler contracts, semantic IR DAGs, context slices, route decisions,
  claim targets, and replay traces;
- architecture gates for training, promotion, self-evolution, resource use,
  safety ledgers, residual escrow, and external-inference boundaries;
- operator work boards, task states, node registries, command vocabulary,
  hooks, feedback routes, TTLs, kill switches, and approval surfaces;
- benchmark ledgers and capability matrices that preserve floors and residuals
  instead of turning local status into general readiness;
- Circle transfer records that can supply deterministic structural receipts
  without becoming model-quality, speed, context-length, or ASI-progress
  evidence.

For future ingestion, these surfaces should be wrapped in a Theseus Report
Packet. A packet should name the originating pressure, contract hash,
work-board item, route, report bundle path, gate result, residual, replay
command, environment boundary, artifact checksum, reviewer decision,
publication permission, missing artifacts, and non-claims.

Only a replayed or otherwise accepted evidence transition should affect
Appendix C. A source note can explain why a design appears in the book. An
imported report can show what a local system recorded. A reproduced run can
support a narrower transition. A missing artifact blocks movement. Those are
different states, and the reader should never have to guess which one is being
used.

## Invariants

Theseus is valuable to the ASI Stack only if it preserves the boundary between
implementation reference and public evidence.

The important invariants are simple:

- reports survive the run and are not replaced by dashboard prose;
- private experiments and public calibration remain separated;
- current-state claims require current artifacts, not historical source notes;
- promotion requires present, passing gate records where the policy demands
  them;
- missing artifacts remain visible;
- source-reported status does not become reproduced evidence by being retold;
- self-evolution proposals follow an intervention ladder instead of inheriting
  trust from momentum.

The live Lean hooks capture a small formal version of this discipline. They
model record-level predicates saying that an implementation-reference claim
needs a report, config, or tool surface rather than dashboard prose alone, and
that capability or self-evolution promotion is blocked when required gate
reports are absent or failing. The report-bundle bridge adds a finite check that
a public-safe audit is accepted only when its bundle components, replay rows,
crosswalk rows, gate mappings, work-board contract fields, artifact gaps,
intervention ladder, and no-promotion boundaries are complete. Those hooks do
not prove that a Theseus run succeeded; they prove only the shape of the gate
the book wants to enforce.

## Failure Modes

The failure modes are the usual ways a prototype story becomes stronger than
its artifacts.

Latest-file reporting can overwrite lineage. Benchmark pressure can turn into
capability theater. Teacher dependence can replace local evidence. Architecture
churn can outrun residual diagnosis. Remote work can proceed without
allowlists, TTLs, or kill switches. A local report can be narrated as public
proof. A dashboard can make missing commands and private artifacts feel
irrelevant.

The most dangerous failure is report-state drift. A historical source note, an
imported static report, a clean rerun, and a current live dashboard are not the
same thing. If the prose treats them as equivalent, the book has laundered its
own evidence.

The antidote is not distrust of prototypes. It is stronger bookkeeping.
Prototype pressure should create better questions: which artifact is missing,
which claim is blocked, which gate needs a replay, which residual owner is
unclear, and which non-claim should remain attached to the next summary.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The minimum viable implementation is a public-safe report crosswalk.

It should map one ASI Stack layer to one Theseus report, config, or tool
surface, then record the evidence state, missing artifacts, public claim
boundary, and non-claims. It should include at least one blocked or missing row
so the system demonstrates refusal, not just acceptance.

The repository already has the first narrow pieces: a crosswalk-style fixture,
static architecture-gate and generation-mode import validators, a support
replay probe over those validators, and a public-safe report-bundle audit. The
audit fixture contains the minimum bundle shape: one goal contract, one
compiler artifact, one work-board item, one gate record, one residual record,
one non-claim, one review note, and one publication boundary. It also keeps
two replay-ready repository rows, one blocked live task-bundle row, eight
crosswalk rows, five gate mappings, six visible artifact gaps, and six
self-evolution intervention-ladder levels in view.

That lets the book show report-first discipline without exposing private
workloads or implying broad capability evidence. The next honest step is a
clean current Theseus replay, archived public release fixture, or public task
bundle with environment notes, publication permissions, artifact digests, and
review boundaries.

## Beyond The State Of The Art

The mature version is a public-safe implementation-reference lab.

In that lab, Theseus reports, configs, gates, ledgers, work-board entries,
residuals, replay notes, and publication boundaries would be importable as
bundles. Dashboards could visualize the bundles, but they would not replace
them. Each bundle would identify the ASI Stack layer it exercises, the command
or report source, configuration, environment boundary, artifact checksum, gate
result, residual, missing artifact, public claim boundary, and non-claims.

A mature report lab would also make failures first-class. Failed replays,
blocked promotions, dirty checkouts, private payload boundaries, stale
dashboards, missing checksums, and inconclusive gates would all remain visible.
That visibility is what would make the lab useful to researchers and ordinary
readers at the same time. It would show not only what the system can do, but
what the system is not yet allowed to say.

At that endpoint, Theseus would be a falsifiable reference for implementation
evidence. Every report would either support a narrow public boundary or
explicitly fail to do so.

## Summary

Project Theseus gives the book a practical implementation reference for
report-first AI systems: pressure, residuals, plan contracts, gates, ledgers,
operator work boards, checkpoints, and self-evolution governance.

The public boundary stays strict. The static architecture-gate and
generation-mode imports show how Theseus reports can be mirrored safely, the
support replay probe shows that the ASI-side validators can be rerun against
tracked artifacts, and the report-bundle audit shows the minimum public-safe
bundle shape plus the visible artifact gaps. None of that becomes a clean live
replay, benchmark result, current dashboard inspection, deployment claim,
training authorization, or support-state promotion.

That caution is the value of the Project Theseus reference. Theseus shows what
kind of evidence the stack should want, while the living book records exactly
what evidence it does and does not have.

## Handoff

Project Theseus shows what report-first implementation evidence should look
like. Prototype Roadmap turns that evidence appetite into dependency order:
source inventory, claim ledgers, schemas, validation, and release discipline
first; contracts, context, jobs, adapters, routing, readiness, benchmarks, and
self-improvement later.
